coredata.dc:title: Detection of ciprofloxacin and pH by carbon dots and rapid, visual sensing analysis

coredata.dc:description: Food safety is a serious issue worldwide and practical detection method is vital for the supervision of food safety. It is necessary to establish efficient and economical methods to detect antibiotics, especially antibiotics in complex systems. This study employs citric acid and m-phenylenediamine to synthesize N, P-codoped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) by a microwave-assisted method. Anhydrous ethanol and phosphoric acid are essential to the properties of N, P-CDs. A “turn-on” fluorescent probe based on N, P-CDs was established for detecting ciprofloxacin (CIP) with detection limit down to 24.2 nm. Semiquantitative test stripe and a PS color detection system for CIP were developed to achieve visual and smart detection. The test stripe is applied to the visual detection of CIP residues in milk and a popular Chinese cuisine, Malatang, for the first time. N, P-CDs can also be used to detect pH in the range of pH 7.5–12.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, P-codoped carbon dots
Ciprofloxacin detection
Fluorescence enhancement
Visual detection
pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of N, P-CDs
Pretreatment of milk and Malatang samples
Selective detection of CIP and detection of pH value
Synthesis and optical properties of CDs with different dopants
Structure and composition of N, P-CDs
Selective detection of CIP by N, P-CDs
Possible response mechanism of N, P-CDs to CIP
Detection of pH value by N, P-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV absorption of CA, MPD and N, P-CDs; (b) UV absorption of N-CDs (without additional dopants), N, P-CDs(with H3PO4 as dopant) and N-CDs-H3PO4 (physical mixture of N-CDs with phosphoric acid); (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, N, P-CDs and N-CDs-H3PO4 (ex = 470 nm); (d) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs with different dopants (top to bottom: none, Na3PO4, H3PO4, H2SO4, HNO3, H3BO4); (e) UV absorption of CDs with different dopants (top to bottom: H3BO4, HNO3, H2SO4, Na3PO4, H3PO4, none).

Optical properties and morphology characterization of N, P-CDs: (a) UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N, P-CDs; (b) Changes in emission of N, P-CDs with excitation wavelength; (c) HR-TEM image of N, P-CDs (insets show the lattice structure with 0.21 nm spacing and the particle size distribution); (d) XRD image of N, P-CDs.

Structure and composition of N, P-CDs: (a) Fourier transform infrared spectrum of N, P-CDs and N-CDs; (b) XPS survey of N, P-CDs; (c) N, P-CDs C1s spectrum; (d) N1s spectrum of N, P-CDs; (E) O1s spectrum of N, P-CDs; (F) P2p spectrum of N, P-CDs (note: N, P-CDs (with H3PO4 as dopant) and N-CDs (without H3PO4 as dopant)).

(a) Fluorescence response spectra of different antibiotics to N, P-CDs; (b) Selectivity of N, P-CDs for CIP; (c) N, P-CDs and N, P-CDs-CIP fluorescence changes of CIP at different pH; (d) Optimal response time of N, P-CDs to CIP.

Detection of N, P-CDs on CIP: (a) Fluorescence spectra and color changes of N, P-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of CIP; (b) Linear relationship between (FF0)/F0 and CIP concentration; (c) Quantitative test stripe of CIP in water; (d) PS color recognition system and linear relationship between B/R and CIP concentration.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs, CIP and N, P-CDs-CIP; (b) Infrared spectra of N, P-CDs and N, P-CDs-CIP; (c) UV absorption of N, P-CDs, CIP and N, P-CDs-CIP.
Detection of Malatang soup by N, P-CDs test stripe: (a) Selectivity of N, P-CDs test stripe for CIP; (b) Sensitivity of N, P- for CIP with different concentrations in Malatang soup.

Detection of pH and mechanism speculation: (a) Fluorescence intensity changes and linear relationship of N, P-CDs at different pH; (b) UV absorption at pH 2 and 12; (c) Fluorescence spectra at pH 2 and 12.

head.ce:title: Detection of ciprofloxacin and pH by carbon dots and rapid, visual sensing analysis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food safety is a serious issue worldwide and practical detection method is vital for the supervision of food safety. It is necessary to establish efficient and economical methods to detect antibiotics, especially antibiotics in complex systems. This study employs citric acid and m-phenylenediamine to synthesize N, P-codoped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) by a microwave-assisted method. Anhydrous ethanol and phosphoric acid are essential to the properties of N, P-CDs. A “turn-on” fluorescent probe based on N, P-CDs was established for detecting ciprofloxacin (CIP) with detection limit down to 24.2 nm. Semiquantitative test stripe and a PS color detection system for CIP were developed to achieve visual and smart detection. The test stripe is applied to the visual detection of CIP residues in milk and a popular Chinese cuisine, Malatang, for the first time. N, P-CDs can also be used to detect pH in the range of pH 7.5–12.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, citric acid (CA) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD), phosphoric acid was used to prepare N, P-CDs by microwave-assisted method and simple purification procedure of dialysis. Without the need of further surface modification and tedious column chromatography separation, the obtained N, P-CDs show bright yellow fluorescence in absolute ethanol (relative quantum yield 43.67%). Based on the fluorescence enhancement effect, N, P-CDs can effectively detect and analyze the content of CIP in complex samples such as milk and spicy hot pot. A low-cost visual test paper based on N, P-CDs was established, which achieved selective detection of CIP through fluorescence color change. In addition, N, P-CDs are also sensitive to pH, which makes them potentially applicable in pH sensing and expands their application areas.

Deionized water was employed as the experimental solvent. Ofloxacin (OFL, 98%), levofloxacin (LEV, 98%), norfloxacin (NOR, 98%), ciprofloxacin (CIP, 98%), enoxacin (ENO, 98%), gatifloxacin (GAT, 98%), metronidazole (MRN, 98%), ornidazole (ORN, 98%), tetracycline (TC, 98%), chloramphenicol (CHL, 98%), trisodium phosphate, ferric nitrate nonahydrate, chromium nitrate nonahydrate, copper nitrate, aluminum nitrate, cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate, zinc chloride, o-phenylenediamine (MPD, 99.5%), L-cysteine (L-cys, 99%), lysine (Lys, 99%), arginine (Arg, 99%), serine (Ser, 99%), asparagine (Asp, 99%), threonine (Thr, 99%) and L-methionine (Met, 99%) were procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), phosphoric acid, boric acid, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, potassium nitrate, sodium fluoride, ammonium chloride, sodium bromide, sodium hydroxide, anhydrous acetic acid, and anhydrous ethanol were obtained from Tianjin Yong Big Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The above chemicals are all AR grade. Sulfuric acid, nitric acid, and mercury hydrochloride were sourced from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shantou, Guangdong, China). Three milk samples were purchased from the supermarket of Hebei University of Science and Technology in Hebei Province, China, with the brands Mengniu, Yili and Junlebao. Three Malatang samples were purchased from 3 canteens on the campus of Hebei University of Science and Technology.

The samples were synthesized using the Opule MD2 microwave digestion instrument (Opule Technology Group Co., LTD, Chengdu, Sichuan, China). Subsequently, a DZ-28C vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Test Instrument Co., LTD, Tianjin, China) was employed for sample drying. The fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots were recorded using the F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., LTD, Shanghai, China). The ultraviolet absorption spectrum was determined using the TU-1900 double beam ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV, Beijing Puyang Universal Instrument Co., LTD, Beijing, China). The morphology of the CDs was visualized through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Infrared spectra were obtained using the Nicolet 6700 Infrared-Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, America). The elemental composition of the CDs was analyzed with the ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, America, Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com)). pH adjustment was carried out using a pen-type acidimeter (Shanghai Lichenbangxi Instrument Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The product was subsequently purified using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane (Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and a 1000 Da dialysis bag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CA served as the carbon source, MPD was used as the nitrogen source, and absolute ethanol was employed as the solvent. Specifically, 0.1 g of CA and 0.1 g of MPD were introduced into a 25 mL beaker containing absolute ethanol, after which the mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min. Subsequently, 5 mL of phosphoric acid was added into the solution. The resulting mixture was then subjected to microwave digestion at 180 °C for 30 min, followed by natural cooling to room temperature. The resulting CDs solution was subjected to centrifugation, and the supernatant was subsequently filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous membrane. The filtered solution was then transferred to a dialysis bag (1000 Da) and dialyzed in deionized water for a duration of 3 days, with periodic water replacement every 8 h. The obtained solution was evaporated and dried at 60 °C to obtain a solid substance. The obtained solid was named N, P-CDs, with a yield of 18.2%.

For comparison, carbon dots without any additional dopants (N-CDs) and with other dopants (Na3PO4, H2SO4, HNO3, H3BO3) were also synthesized under the same conditions, denoted by Na3PO4-CDs, H2SO4-CDs, HNO3-CDs, H3BO3-CDs, respectively.

The UV spectrum of the sample was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer and the relative quantum yield was calculated using quinine sulfate as the reference substance (see Supporting Information). Subsequently, the infrared spectrum of the sample was measured by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer to analyze its functional groups. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer and the optimal excitation wavelength was determined. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphology of CDs. Finally, the elemental composition of CDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of milk and Malatang samples
Selective detection of CIP and detection of pH value
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs were measured at difference excitation wavelengths and the optimal excitation was determined as 470 nm.

The detection performance of N, P-CDs for antibiotics was examined using OFL, LEV, NOR, ENO, GAT, MRN, ORN, TC, CHL, CIP, L-cys, Lys, Arg, Ser, Asp, Thr, Met, Na+, NH4 +, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cl−, F−, Br−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, and NO3 − as detection candidates. At room temperature, 0.5 mL of 100 μM antibiotic solution and 4 mL of N, P-CDs solution were mixed for 5 min, and the fluorescence intensity was measured and recorded. After that, 1 mL of N, P-CDs-CIP solution was mixed with 4 mL of interfering substance solution, the fluorescence intensity was measured and recorded to test anti-interference ability of N, P-CDs.

0.5 mL of deionized water-HCl-NaOH solutions with different pH values was added to 4.5 mL of N, P-CDs solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was measured at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm to determine the optimal pH value

0.5 mL of 0–30 μM CIP solution was mixed with 4.5 mL of N, P-CDs solution then the mixture was adjusted to pH 8. The fluorescence intensity was recorded to form a linear fluorescence intensity-concentration relationship. The limit of detection was then calculated following the equation LOD = 3σ/k.

The feasibility of N, P-CDs for CIP was proved by recovery experiment in milk and Malatang samples. Milk samples containing CIP standard solutions of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 3.0, and 6.0 μM, and Malatang samples containing CIP standard solutions of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 3.0, 5.0, and 7.0 μM were prepared. The fluorescence intensity of milk sample and Malatang sample was measured and used to calculate the concentration of CIP, and recovery rate.

The detection of pH value was based on the variation of fluorescence intensity with changing pH value in the range of pH 7.5–12. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs under different pH conditions was recorded to form a linear fluorescence intensity-pH relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of CDs with different dopants
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the role of phosphoric acid in forming N, P-CDs, control experiments were further conducted. Cyan carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by microwave digestion of the CA and MPD at 180 °C for 30 min without adding phosphoric acid (Fig. S3). Then we measured the fluorescence spectrum and UV absorption of N-CDs for comparison with N, P- CDs and the physical mixture of N-CDs with phosphoric acid (N-CDs-H3PO4). The UV and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, N, P-CDs and N-CDs-H3PO4 were compared. The UV–Vis absorption bands of N-CDs and N-CDs-H3PO4 are mainly located at 200–280 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. By contrast, N, P-CDs show an obvious new absorption peak at 444 nm, which is likely to contribute to the yellow light emission of N, P-CDs (Fig. 1b). The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-H3PO4 is much lower than that of N-CDs and N, P-CDs (Fig. 1c), indicating that N, P-CDs are not simply a mixture of N-CDs and phosphoric acid. Phosphoric acid participates in forming of N, P-CDs.

The morphology and particle size of N, P-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2c and its inset, N, P-CDs are well monodispersed spherical nanoparticles, and their size distribution is mainly concentrated between 2 and 9 nm, with an average particle size of about 4.7 nm. The HR-TEM results in the inset show that it has a clear lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. Subsequently, the XRD patterns of N, P-CDs were characterized by X-ray diffractometer. Fig. 2d shows that a broad peak appeared at 2θ of 22.97°, which may be attributed to the graphite carbon (002) plane and reflects the existence of a layered structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of N, P-CDs
Selective detection of CIP by N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most common quinolone antibiotics, CIP is difficult to degrade in nature and harmful to the environment and human health. Therefore, the sensitive and selective detection of CIP is of great significance. The selectivity of N, P-CDs towards CIP was evaluated. As can be seen from Fig. 4a, CIP significantly increases the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs, while other antibiotics have no significant effect on its emission of N, P-CDs. At the same time, other common ions and amino acids do not show obvious fluorescence response to the carbon dots (Fig. S7). In addition, it can also be seen from the bar graph that CIP significantly changes the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs (Fig. 4b).

To examine the working condition of N, P-CDs probe, we first examined the stability of N, P-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs basically remains in 20 d at room temperature, indicating that it can be stored at room temperature.Then we examined the effect of ion strength, temperature, pH on fluorescence of N, P-CDs.The effect of NaCl solutions (0–5 M) on the fluorescence of N, P-CDs is almost negligible (Fig. S5), indicating good salt stability. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs at different temperatures suggests that highest fluorescence intensity is achieved at room temperature (Fig. S6). In addition, We tested the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs in the pH range of 2–13. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of both N, P-CDs show steady and small increase from pH 4 to 8. The increase was obvious at pH 8–12, reaching the maximum value at pH 12, and then decreased at pH 13. This shows that N, P-CDs are sensitive to pH. Studies have shown that changes in fluorescence properties caused by pH are affected by the protonation and deprotonation processes(Hu et al., 2021). The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs-CIP also tended to be stable in the pH range of 4–8. For the purpose of high emission, environmental protection and simple operation, pH 8 was finally selected for the detection system (towards CIP). We also examined response time. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs gradually increased within 0–5 min after adding CIP, and the fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged after 5 min. Therefore, we finally chose pH 8 and a response time of 5 min as the optimal detection conditions.

We also noticed fluorescence color of N, P-CDs also gradually changes from yellow-green to blue as the added CIP concentration increases(Fig. 5a), which inspired us to develop portal test stripe. We cut the filter paper into stripe of 1 cm × 3 cm, and immersed it in N, P-CDs solution for 5 min. Then the stripe is dried in an oven to prepare N, P-CDs test stripe. CIP solutions of different concentrations was dropped on the test stripe and the color changes can be observed with a handheld UV light (365 nm UV light). It can be seen from Fig. 5c that as the CIP concentration changes from 0 to 30 μM, the fluorescence color of the test strip gradually changes from yellow to blue. Therefore, this test strip has very good application prospects in testing the CIP concentration and quantitatively determining CIP. In order to develop a more convenient detection method, we developed the PS color recognition system to generate color code. By fitting the relationship between blue channel/red channel (B/R) and CIP concentration, we obtained a linear correlation model. As shown in Fig. 5d, in the range of 0.2–20 μM, B/R shows a good linear relationship with CIP concentration. This provides the potential for more intelligent detection. The application of the PS color recognition system allows the detection results to be visually presented through color code. Meanwhile, the linear relationship between the B/R ratio and the CIP concentration allows accurately and quantitatively determine the concentration of CIP within a certain range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of N, P-CDs to CIP

ce:section.ce:para: Under the above optimal detection conditions, we added standard solutions of CIP with varying concentrations into the milk for real sample detection. Recovery rate was calculated to verify the accuracy and feasibility of the method. As shown in Table S2, among the three milk samples, the recovery rate of CIP ranged from 97.47% to 102.43%, and the relative standard deviation ranged from 1.23% to 4.87%. This shows that N, P-CDs are feasible for detection of CIP in real samples and are expected to be used as an economical and environmentally friendly carbon nanosensor to quantitatively detect CIP.

Malatang, as a well-known Chinese delicacy, has a variety of ingredients, including meat, eggs, vegetables, seafood, etc. However, some unscrupulous pedlars may use substandard ingredients or illegal added antibiotics in Malatang. Although there is a need for detection CIP for food safety, to the best of our knowledge, there is currently no method available for the rapid detection of CIP in Malatang. In this study, we used the prepared N, P-CDs test stripe to perform on-site quick detection of CIP in Malatang soup.

First, we filter the Malatang soup to remove large pieces of solid ingredients and perform preprocessing according to section 2.5. Different antibiotics were dissolved in the pretreated Malatang soup to prepare 1 μM antibiotics solutions. Then the solution was dropped on the test stripe and the fluorescent color of the test stripe under UV light was observed. As shown in Fig. 7a, CIP-soaked stripe turns from yellow to green, while the color of other test stripes observe remains yellow. This shows that the N, P-CDs test stripe can achieve detection for CIP in Malatang sample. Then we completely immersed the test stripe into a series of Malatang soups with different concentrations of CIP (0–10 μM) and observe the color of the stripes under UV light. As depicted in Fig. 7b, with the rise of CIP concentration, the color of the test strip's fluorescence progressively transitions from yellow to blue. This demonstrates that the N, P-CDs test stripe we have fabricated can effectively quantified for the visual detection of CIP in real samples by fluorescence color changes. In addition, we also measured the fluorescence of Malatang soup containing CIP solutions within the linear detection range (0.5–25 μM) and calculated the recovery rate. The recovery rates fall within the range of 95.69% to 104.43%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD %) varies from 1.81% to 4.96% (Table S3), affirming the feasibility of the detection method. The practical study may provide a feasible new method for the authorities to monitor food safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of pH value by N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We have noticed that the increment of fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs at pH 7.5–12 at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm (Fig. 8a), indicating that N, P-CDs are highly sensitive to pH values. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity has a good linear relationship with pH value (R2 = 0.9911). Therefore, N, P-CDs can be used for pH value detection under alkaline conditions. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs is reversible between pH 7.5 and 12 (as depicted in Fig. S7), indicating their reusability. Subsequently, we further summarized some carbon dots-based pH probes for comparison and reference (Table S4). This part of the content aims to provide a broader perspective so that readers can have a more comprehensive understanding of the different applications and performance of carbon dots in pH-sensitive detection.

In this research, N, P-CDs were synthesized through a straightforward and eco-friendly microwave-assisted approach, employing CA and MPD as precursors, and introducing phosphoric acid as a dopant. The investigation focused on the impact of phosphoric acid on the optical properties of CDs. A CIP detection method based on “turn on” mode was established and verified. Which is more efficient, convenient and economical than other methods for detecting CIP. The prepared test stripe can realize the visual detection of CIP in Malatang, provide a pioneeringly feasible new method for the authorities to monitor food safety. Simultaneously, N, P-CDs is effective for pH detection applications. These excellent results make N, P-CDs have considerable prospects and potential application in environment, food, and diagnosis fields.

Dao-Han Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Liang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Nan Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Kun Su: Writing-reviewing&editing, Supervision. Lei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Chun-Yan Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Key Project of Biomedicine Joint Fund of Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2020208043). Hebei innovation center of pharmaceutical and chemical engineering (225676121H).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.140313.
